skill.name: scrna-cell-type-annotator
skill.description: Auto-annotate cell clusters from single-cell RNA data using marker genes.
skill.summary: Automatically identify cell types from scRNA-seq cluster data by matching differentially expressed marker genes against a curated reference database.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 38 static analysis findings are false positives. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings match non-cryptographic gene marker names (e.g., CD3D, CD4). The 'system reconnaissance' findings match benign documentation text. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are standard Markdown code blocks showing example commands like 'python scripts/main.py --help'. The path traversal findings are security checklist items advocating prevention, not actual traversal. This is a legitimate bioinformatics skill with a simple, readable Python script for scRNA-seq cell type annotation. No malicious patterns, prompt injection, or data exfiltration detected.

content.user_title: Annotate Cell Types from Single-Cell RNA Sequencing Data
content.value_statement: Bioinformatics researchers spend significant time manually identifying cell clusters from single-cell RNA data. This skill automates cell type annotation by matching cluster marker genes to a curated database and returning ranked predictions with confidence scores.
actual_capabilities: Match cluster marker genes to known cell types using a curated reference database
Score and rank cell type predictions by marker gene overlap proportion
Annotate multiple cell clusters simultaneously from single-cell RNA data
Support immune cell types including T cells, B cells, monocytes, NK cells, and dendritic cells
Generate reproducible annotation reports with confidence levels per prediction
Provide alternative cell type suggestions when primary predictions have low confidence
limitations: Cell type marker database is limited to 6 immune cell types (T cells, B cells, monocytes, NK cells, dendritic cells)
Does not support species-specific marker gene matching or cross-species comparison
Cannot process raw sequencing data or count matrices directly as input
Requires pre-computed cluster marker genes provided by the user as input

use_cases.title: Post-Clustering Cell Type Annotation
use_cases.description: Assign cell type labels to clusters after completing dimensionality reduction and clustering on scRNA-seq data. Input the differentially expressed genes per cluster and receive ranked cell type predictions.

use_cases.title: Cross-Study Cell Type Validation
use_cases.description: Compare cell type annotations across multiple single-cell datasets to verify consistency. Use the standardized marker gene matching approach to validate findings from different experiments.

use_cases.title: Educational Cell Type Identification
use_cases.description: Learn how cell type annotation works by comparing cluster marker genes against known reference panels. Understand the relationship between gene expression patterns and cell identity.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Cluster Annotation
prompt_templates.prompt: I have scRNA-seq cluster data with the following differentially expressed genes per cluster. Cluster 0: CD3D, CD4, IL7R, LTB. Cluster 1: CD79A, CD79B, MS4A1. Cluster 2: CD14, LYZ, S100A9. Please annotate these clusters using the scrna-cell-type-annotator skill and provide the ranked predictions with confidence scores.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user with pre-computed cluster markers needing automated annotation

prompt_templates.title: Low Confidence Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Annotate these clusters but flag any predictions with a confidence score below 0.5 for manual review. My clusters are: Cluster 0: CD3D, CD4, IL7R. Cluster 1: FCER1A, CST3, CLEC10A. Cluster 2: NKG7, GNLY, KLRD1. Provide the full ranking for each cluster.
prompt_templates.scenario: Researcher verifying annotation quality before downstream analysis

prompt_templates.title: Cross-Dataset Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: I have two scRNA-seq experiments on peripheral blood mononuclear cells. Dataset A clusters: Cluster 0: CD14, LYZ. Cluster 1: CD3D, CD8A. Dataset B clusters: Cluster 0: CD3D, CD4. Cluster 1: CD14, S100A9. Annotate both datasets and compare the cell type assignments between them.
prompt_templates.scenario: Bioinformatician validating cell type assignments across replicate experiments

prompt_templates.title: Custom Panel Extension with Fallback
prompt_templates.prompt: Annotate Cluster 0: CD3D, CD4, FOXP3 and Cluster 1: CD3D, CD8A, GZMB using the standard marker database. For any cluster where no cell type exceeds a 0.5 confidence threshold, describe the closest matching cell type and suggest additional marker genes to investigate for confirmation.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user working with regulatory T cell markers needing expanded analysis

output_examples.input: Annotate clusters with markers: Cluster 0: CD3D, CD4, IL7R, LTB. Cluster 1: CD79A, CD79B, MS4A1. Cluster 2: CD14, LYZ, S100A9.
output: CD4 T cell: 1.00 (all 3 markers matched: CD3D, CD4, IL7R)
CD8 T cell: 0.33 (1 of 3 markers matched: CD3D)
B cell: 1.00 (all 3 markers matched: CD79A, CD79B, MS4A1)
Monocyte: 1.00 (all 3 markers matched: CD14, LYZ, S100A9)
Dendritic cell: 0.33 (1 of 3 markers matched: CST3)

output_examples.input: Annotate Cluster 0: NKG7, GNLY, KLRD1 and explain what these gene markers indicate.
output: NK cell: 1.00 (all 3 markers matched: NKG7, GNLY, KLRD1)
CD8 T cell: 0.33 (1 of 3 markers matched: CD8B)

Explanation: NKG7, GNLY (granulysin), and KLRD1 are canonical markers for natural killer cells. NKG7 is involved in cytotoxic granule exocytosis. GNLY is an antimicrobial protein in cytotoxic granules. KLRD1 forms part of the NK cell receptor complex. This combination is highly specific for NK cell identity.

best_practices: Verify that input marker genes are true differentially expressed genes from your clustering analysis, not all genes expressed in the cluster
Review low-confidence predictions manually using domain expertise and known tissue-specific marker literature
Cross-reference annotations with at least one additional cell type identification method for critical applications
anti_patterns: Relying solely on automated annotation without biological validation for novel or rare cell populations
Using the tool for cell types outside the curated marker database without acknowledging the limitation
Feeding raw gene expression matrices or count data instead of pre-computed cluster marker gene lists

faq.question: What input format does the scRNA cell type annotator require?
faq.answer: The skill accepts cluster marker genes as input. You provide a list of differentially expressed genes for each cluster. The tool then matches these against a curated database of known cell type markers.

faq.question: What cell types are in the reference marker database?
faq.answer: The database currently includes CD4 T cells, CD8 T cells, B cells, monocytes, NK cells, and dendritic cells. These cover common immune cell types found in blood and tissue samples.

faq.question: How are confidence scores calculated?
faq.answer: Confidence scores are calculated as the proportion of reference marker genes for a cell type that are present in the cluster marker list. A score of 1.0 means all reference markers matched.

faq.question: Can I run the Python script directly outside of Claude?
faq.answer: Yes. The scripts/main.py file can be run with python scripts/main.py --demo to see example output. Install pandas with pip install -r requirements.txt first.

faq.question: Does this skill work with non-human data?
faq.answer: The current version does not have species-specific marker gene matching. The database uses human gene symbols. Cross-species analysis requires manual gene symbol conversion and validation.

faq.question: How is this different from automated cell type annotation tools like CellTypist or SingleR?
faq.answer: This skill provides a lightweight, prompt-driven interface for cell type annotation within an AI assistant workflow. It complements existing tools but does not replace specialized bioinformatics packages for large-scale analysis.
